ontology.foodex2_description: Miso paste — fermented soybean paste used as seasoning

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of miso paste is approximately 1 tablespoon (17 g), commonly dissolved in hot water or broth to prepare miso soup or used as a marinade or seasoning base.

nutrient_source.title: Miso paste — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for miso paste (F10060040). Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entry for miso (FDC ID 174272) for plausibility validation. HPB values used as primary source per source priority policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for miso paste was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 33 is estimated from the closest analogous food: fermented soybean-based condiments and whole soybean preparations, which consistently show GI values in the range 15–40 in peer-reviewed literature (Brand-Miller et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Miso is predominantly protein and fat with moderate complex carbohydrate (14.5 g/100g), and fermentation further reduces glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (33 × 2.47) / 100 ≈ 0.8 (based on 17 g serving containing approximately 2.47 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content (4300 mg/100g), far exceeding the 600 mg/100g clinical threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should strictly limit miso paste to no more than 1 teaspoon (approximately 5–6 g) per serving, preferably diluted in large volumes of liquid such as soup broth, and should choose low-sodium miso varieties where available to reduce cardiovascular sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~33), high protein (17.2 g/100g), moderate complex carbohydrate (14.5 g/100g), and fermentation-derived bioactive compounds that may support insulin sensitivity via gut microbiome modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Miso paste is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when used in small culinary quantities (1–2 tsp per serving) as a flavouring agent; its low GI and high protein content minimise postprandial glucose excursions, but sodium content must be monitored concurrently.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dual profile: very high sodium (4300 mg/100g) is a significant cardiovascular risk factor, partially offset by soy isoflavones, low saturated fat (1.9 g/100g), zero cholesterol, and high potassium (930 mg/100g) which supports vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use miso paste sparingly (no more than 1 tsp per day) due to its extreme sodium content, and should prioritise low-sodium miso formulations; the soy isoflavone and potassium content may offer modest cardioprotective benefit when intake is tightly controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fermented food with probiotic-like activity supporting gut-immune axis relevant to RA, but very high sodium (4300 mg/100g) may exacerbate inflammatory tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Miso paste may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit via gut microbiome modulation and soy isoflavones, but must be used in very small quantities due to its extreme sodium content, which can worsen RA-associated fluid retention and systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Miso paste is a traditional Japanese fermented seasoning produced by fermenting soybeans with koji (Aspergillus oryzae mould culture), salt, and water, and optionally with grains such as rice or barley, over periods ranging from weeks to several years. The result is a thick, dense, intensely savoury paste with a deep umami flavour profile, ranging in colour from pale yellow-white (shiro miso) to deep reddish-brown (aka miso) depending on fermentation duration and ingredient ratios. Miso is a nutritionally dense condiment, providing 217 kcal per 100 g, with a notably high protein content of 17.2 g/100g derived from soybean amino acids, moderate fat at 10.5 g/100g (predominantly polyunsaturated), and moderate carbohydrate at 14.5 g/100g. Its most clinically significant characteristic is its extremely high sodium content of 4300 mg/100g, which makes it unsuitable for unrestricted use in hypertension or cardiovascular disease management. However, miso is typically used in small culinary quantities — 1 to 2 teaspoons per serving — dissolved in soup broth or used as a glaze or marinade, substantially reducing per-serving sodium exposure. Miso is a rich source of iron (6.8 mg/100g), calcium (150 mg/100g), potassium (930 mg/100g), and zinc (2.6 mg/100g). As a fermented food, miso contains live microbial cultures and bioactive compounds including soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) that may support gut microbiome health, immune regulation, and insulin sensitivity. Its glycemic index is estimated at approximately 33 (low), making it appropriate for blood glucose management when used in controlled portions. Miso is a cornerstone ingredient in Japanese cuisine, used in miso soup (misoshiru), ramen broth, marinades for fish and meat (saikyo-yaki), salad dressings, and glazes. It is plant-based, vegan, and dairy-free, though it contains soy allergens and is not gluten-free when barley or wheat is used in production.
common_user_queries: Is miso paste good for diabetes?
How much sodium is in miso paste?
Can I eat miso paste if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of miso?
Is miso paste high in protein?
What is miso paste made from?
How many calories in miso paste?
What is the difference between white miso and red miso?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F10060040) with 0.95 confidence rating. Values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central miso entry (FDC ID 174272) used for cross-validation. Fatty acid breakdown, B-vitamins, and mineral micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central where HPB did not report detailed sub-fractions. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature (Brand-Miller et al., Diabetes Care 2008) as no direct miso GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; this is documented and does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the strong compositional basis for the estimate.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.9 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (2.3 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (5.9 g/100g), trans fat (0.0 g/100g), sugar (5.0 g/100g), and selected B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6) and minerals (phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174272) as HPB did not report these sub-fractions. This is documented as an adapted source match. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not consistently reported for miso paste in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the level of precision required; available values vary significantly by miso variety and fermentation method and cannot be reliably assigned to a generic miso paste entry. 3. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100g — confirmed zero per both HPB and USDA FoodData Central; miso is a plant-derived fermented food containing no animal-derived cholesterol. 4. GI of 33 is an analogous estimate based on fermented soybean food category data from Brand-Miller et al. (Diabetes Care, 2008) and the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for miso paste was located. 5. Halal status is marked false as traditional miso fermentation may involve alcohol-producing fermentation byproducts; halal-certified miso exists but cannot be assumed for a generic entry. 6. Gluten-free status is marked false as many miso varieties include barley or wheat; rice-based miso (shiro miso) may be gluten-free but this cannot be assumed for a generic entry.
